title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202512023H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-18460

CPA HEARING NO. 119,718

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:  December 1, 2013 THROUGH March 31, 2017


Direct Payment/RDT
 

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


ALBERTO ALEJANDRO CRUZ

Representing Respondent

 

**************

Representing Petitioner


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on Jauary 23, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as changed.[2] 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as changed is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a zero amount due.

 
SIGNED on this 29th day of December 2025

 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as changed

 

 


 

ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-25-18460                                                   

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 119,718

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) audited ************** (Petitioner) for sales and use tax compliance and issued an assessment consisting of tax and interest. Penalty was waived. Petitioner requested redetermination for various reasons and provided additional documentation. The adjustments resulted in a credit due; however, disputes remain. Petitioner contends it is due additional credits for tax paid or accrued in error for items eligible for the manufacturing exemption, nontaxable scheduled and periodic maintenance for turnaround services, new construction, and hazardous waste removal or collection services. Staff contends additional adjustments are not warranted. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends that the amended assessment be affirmed.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) on May 2, 2025. On May 6, 2025, ALJ Keneshia Washington issued an Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits and Setting Pre-Hearing Requirements for a hearing to be held in person at the SOAH offices in Austin. On May 7, 2025, Staff issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing. On September 17, 2025, the ALJ issued an order granting Petitioner’s unopposed motion to convert the hearing to written submission and cancelling the in-person hearing. Petitioner was represented by ************** of COMPANY Alberto Alejandro Cruz represented Staff. The contested case record closed on October 20, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence Presented

 

Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged by the parties while the case was in redetermination at the Comptroller and filed the following exhibits for the written submission hearing:


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty & Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Report;
	
	
	Audit Plan;
	
	
	Amended Audit Report; and
	
	
	Amended Audit Plan.
	


 

Petitioner offered the following exhibits:

 


	
	Petitioner’s Turnaround Schedule;
	
	
	Petitioner’s Reply to Position Letter; and
	
	
	Petitioner’s Request for Redetermination and Refund.
	


 

The parties’ exhibits were admitted to the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff did not agree to adjust the amended assessment.

 

C. Facts Established and Issues Presented

 

Petitioner is a foreign limited liability company based in CITY, Texas. During the period at issue, Petitioner operated as a global manufacturer and marketer of differentiated and specialty chemicals. During the audit, Petitioner’s tax manager described the factors considered when scheduling turnaround services regarding the catalysts. He explained that as Petitioner makes product, the catalyst ages so that Petitioner must run the catalyst a little hotter to maintain production. Eventually the catalyst will reach a temperature limit so that Petitioner can no longer maintain target production. Holding at the maximum temperature, production will steadily decline. At this point, the catalyst is deemed to have reached the end of its useful life. Petitioner then decides whether to turnaround the catalyst or, for market reasons, elect to continue to produce less than the target production to stretch out the turnaround cycle to meet business goals. The tax manager identified conditions that could prolong the turnaround cycle by six months, including increasing the production rate for business goals, operating at non-optimum conditions, and emergency shutdowns where the catalyst is not kept warm. He also identified business decisions that affect the turnaround cycle, such as an increase in the cost of raw materials, coordination of shutdowns with other equipment units, low product demand, and planned shutdowns due to required maintenance on safety equipment.

 

In June 2017, Staff initiated a direct payment sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of December 1, 2013, through March 31, 2017. Petitioner provided the auditor with financial statements, purchase invoices, tax accrual worksheets, tax worksheets, general ledgers, and a chart of accounts. Due to the volume of documents and consideration of the procedure from the previous audit, the auditor used a sample procedure to conduct the audit. Petitioner’s documentation reliably separated taxed and non-taxed populations. The auditor evaluated purchases through five separate sample groups: inventory, taxed labor, non-taxed labor, taxed assets and expenses, and non-taxed assets and expenses. The auditor selected sample purchases from the groups and reviewed supporting documents, including purchase invoices and work papers. In multiple sample exams, he scheduled taxable purchases for which Petitioner did not remit tax to the vendor or accrue tax to remit to the Comptroller. He also scheduled credits where applicable.

 

On May 28, 2021, Staff issued Petitioner a Texas Notification of Audit Results assessing tax and interest. Staff waived penalty. Petitioner timely requested redetermination of the audit. Staff amended the audit by removing items for exempt manufacturing equipment, exempt railcars, nontaxable railroad switching services, and exempt waste removal services. The auditor also agreed to credit taxed transactions for exempt manufacturing equipment, new construction labor, and nontaxable consulting services. The amendments to the assessment resulted in a credit of tax and corresponding interest. However, disputes remain.

 

Petitioner contends it is due additional credits for tax paid or accrued in error for items eligible for the manufacturing exemption, nontaxable periodic and scheduled turnaround services, new construction, and hazardous waste removal or collection services. Staff maintains Petitioner is not entitled to any additional refunds or credits and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. ALJ’s Analysis and Recommendation

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. A “sale” includes a transfer of title or possession of tangible personal property or the performance of a taxable service when done or performed for consideration. Id. § 151.005(1), (3). All components of the sale of taxable personal property are part of the total sales price and are taxable, and the total amount for which a taxable item is sold, leased, or rented includes a service that is a part of the sale. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.007(a), (b).

 

Real property repair and remodeling services are taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0101(a)(13). With certain exceptions, real property repair and remodeling means the repair, restoration, remodeling, or modification of an improvement to real property. 151.0047(a). The term “contractor” includes any person who makes improvements to real property as part of periodic and scheduled maintenance of nonresidential real property. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(2). For operational and functional improvements to realty, maintenance means scheduled, periodic work that is necessary to sustain or support safe, efficient, continuous operations, or to prevent the decline, failure, lapse, or deterioration of the improvement. Id. § 3.357(a)(7). Taxable real property services that are described by Texas Administrative Code § 3.356 (relating to Real Property Service) do not qualify as maintenance. Id. Maintenance does not include work to remodel, modify, upgrade, perform major repair, or restore, even if the work is scheduled or periodic. Id. As it relates to maintenance, the term “scheduled” means anticipated and designated to occur within a given time period or production level. Id. § 3.357(a)(7)(A). As it relates to maintenance, the term “periodic” means ongoing or continual or at least occurring at intervals of time or production that are reasonably predictable. Id. § 3.357(a)(7)(B). The scheduled shutdown or turnaround of a manufacturing or processing plant is considered to be maintenance within the meaning of this definition. Id. § 3.357(a)(7)(C). Maintenance on real property is a nontaxable service. Id. § 3.357(d)(2).

 

To qualify a purchase as nontaxable real property maintenance, a service provider’s customer must prove, by way of maintenance schedules or work orders or other similar forms of evidence, that the services meet the definition of maintenance on real property that is stated in subsection (a)(7) of this section. Id. § 3.357(d)(2)(A). If the service provider does not have a written contract, but is only hired on a per job basis, then the service provider must presume that the service is repair or restoration and must therefore collect tax. Id. If the customer has documentation to prove that the service qualifies as maintenance, then the customer may issue to the service provider an exemption certificate in lieu of paying tax or provide the documentation required to overcome the presumption. Id. The certificate must state that the labor is maintenance as defined in subsection (a)(7) of this section, rather than repair or restoration as defined in subsection (a)(13) and (15) of this section, and that the customer is liable for any additional tax that is due in the event that the Comptroller determines that a taxable service was performed. Id.

 

The Texas Court of Appeals considered whether a service met the definition of scheduled and periodic maintenance in GATX Terminals Corp. v. Rylander, 78 S.W.3d 630 (Tex. App.—Austin 2002, no pet.). In that case, the taxpayer asserted that it purchased tank repainting services to occur on a scheduled and periodic basis and, to support its assertion, presented the testimony of its regional facility engineering manager that the interval of the repainting service was based on the ten-year life of the paint job. The Comptroller argued that the repainting was not scheduled or periodic and presented the testimony of the taxpayer’s purchasing manager that the taxpayer’s tank repainting was an on-going project without a formal written policy or any documented schedule and that the decision to paint a tank lies with the terminal manager, terminal engineer, and maintenance manager. The Comptroller also noted that the taxpayer classified the repainting services at issue as a “special project,” and the taxpayer’s books showed that such special projects were performed regularly but not necessarily daily, monthly, or quarterly such that expenses associated with these special projects are not consistent because of variables such as the size and the number of tanks involved. The appellate court found that “the record is replete with evidence that indicates that the tanks are repainted only as needed,” that “the decision to repaint a tank is largely made at the discretion of GATX managers and not by strictly objective standards,” and that “subjective factors, independent of the condition of the paint, play a significant role in determining the frequency of repainting the tanks.” GATX, 78 S.W.3d at 637. Based on record evidence, the appellate court held that the repainting services were taxable repair and remodeling services that were not excluded from tax as scheduled and periodic maintenance.

 

The appellate court considered Comptroller’s Decisions interpreting the meaning of the terms scheduled and periodic when making its determination and summarized that the decisions have held that scheduled work is arranged in advance rather than on an as-needed basis and that work that is prompted by a subjective judgment is not periodic. GATX, 78 S.W.3d at 635, referencing Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 30,911 (1999) and 28,468 (1992). Furthermore, the Comptroller has held that the scheduled shutdown or turnaround of a manufacturing or processing plant is generally considered to be maintenance of real property, but repairs or restoration performed during a scheduled, periodic shutdown are considered major repair (and hence not maintenance) if performed on improvements that were not operational when the shutdown occurred. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 40,239, 40,911 (2004). Guidance from the Comptroller’s Tax Policy Division explained that working on a unit during the same month in two subsequent years does not per se support a determination that the work was scheduled and periodic; however, the Comptroller may allow minor variances due to such things as severe weather; yet wider variances may support an indication that the services were not scheduled or periodic. State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document No. 200710980L (October 8, 2007).

 

Additional Tax Policy guidance explains, “There are no specific requirements for the amount of time or production between turnarounds.” STAR Document No. 202407003M (July 1220, 2024). Furthermore, “Although the intervals between turnarounds must be reasonably predictable, turnarounds may often be delayed or pushed back. For example, a functioning refinery may push back a scheduled turnaround to maintain production after other refineries have been damaged by a hurricane.” Id. To support a contention that purchases are scheduled and periodic turnaround services, a taxpayer should provide supporting documentation such as scope of work documents, project plans, turnaround schedules, vendor contracts, invoices, and Authorization for Expenditures (AFEs), which are internal approvals for expenses from a taxpayer’s capital expenditure budget. Id.

 

The Comptroller shall refund or credit tax if he determines that tax was unlawfully or erroneously collected. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a). When a refund claim is filed, the claimant is arguing that tax was “unlawfully or erroneously collected,” and the burden of proof rests on the claimant to prove the matter asserted, since the refund claim is based on documents in the claimant’s possession. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 43,649 and 43,665 (2006).

 

The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b). For example, a taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3). In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a). A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012). In addition, a taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,078 (2015).

 

In the present case, Petitioner purchased services that it categorized as turnaround services in its books. The auditor removed purchases where contracts and other supporting documentation showed that the services were scheduled and periodic during the audit and made additional adjustments when he amended the audit. The auditor did not exclude the turnaround purchases at issue after determining that the purchases were not “periodic” because catalyst change periods were not based on calendar date, machine hours, or another measurement that would render the services predictable. The auditor found that the evidence showed that the catalyst was changed based on a combination of factors beginning with time, then production, and the condition of the catalyst.

 

Petitioner contends the turnaround services at issue are scheduled and periodic because the predominant factor in the turnaround schedule of the catalyst is its useful life, and any deviations from the schedule are based on inclement weather or corresponding reductions in production. To support its contention, Petitioner provided summary schedules showing dates when the turnarounds occurred for various units along with notes regarding variances, such as Hurricane Harvey, inclement weather, as well as economic and production reasons.

 

Staff argues that record evidence establishes that some combination of factors that affect the intervals of the turnaround services renders them not scheduled or periodic. Record evidence supports Staff’s position. Specifically, documentation provided during redetermination and communications from Petitioner’s tax manager show that, even though Petitioner’s decision to initiate catalyst turnaround services may begin with considering the useful life of the catalyst, other factors, such as production goals and the cost of raw production materials, ultimately determine the turnaround intervals. The impact of the variables prevents the turnaround intervals from being predictable. Petitioner’s schedules are not supported by underlying source records, rendering them tantamount to bare assertions. Bare assertions are insufficient to demonstrate error in the audit. See Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.—Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); Comptroller’s Decision No. 114,880 (2019). Furthermore, summary schedules and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012). Based on record evidence, Staff met its burden to establish that Petitioner purchased taxable real property repair and remodeling services, and Petitioner has not met its evidentiary burden to show that the services constituted scheduled and periodic maintenance that would be excludable from tax. Petitioner’s contention should be denied.

 

Petitioner also contends that it is due additional credits for purchases eligible for the manufacturing exemption and purchases of hazardous waste removal services excluded from tax. Petitioner contends that it provided sufficient documentation to support these contentions; however, as noted above, Petitioner did not submit evidence in support of these contentions in the written submission hearing, and assertions in pleadings are not sufficient for Petitioner to meet its evidentiary burden. Therefore, the contentions should be denied.

 

The notice of hearing references other contentions that appear to be rendered moot based on the amendments in the audit and Petitioner’s written submission brief. The ALJ finds that Petitioner has not provided sufficient documentation to support any additional credits and recommends that the amended assessment be upheld.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	************** (Petitioner) is a foreign limited liability company based in CITY, Texas. During the period at issue, Petitioner operated as a global manufacturer and marketer of differentiated and specialty chemicals.
	
	
	During the audit, Petitioner’s tax manager described the factors considered when scheduling turnaround services regarding the catalysts. He explained that as Petitioner makes product, the catalyst ages so that Petitioner must run the catalyst a little hotter to maintain production. Eventually the catalyst will reach a temperature limit so that Petitioner can no longer maintain target production. Holding at the maximum temperature, production will steadily decline. At this point, the catalyst is deemed to have reached the end of its useful life.
	
	
	Petitioner then decides whether to turnaround the catalyst or, for market reasons, elect to continue to produce less than the target production to stretch out the turnaround cycle to meet business goals. The tax manager identified conditions that could prolong the turnaround cycle by six months, including increasing the production rate for business goals, operating at non-optimum conditions, and emergency shutdowns where the catalyst is not kept warm.
	
	
	Petitioner’s tax manager identified business decisions that affect the turnaround cycle, such as an increase in the cost of raw materials, coordination of shutdowns with other equipment units, low product demand, and planned shutdowns due to required maintenance on safety equipment.
	
	
	In June 2017, the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) initiated a direct payment sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of December 1, 2013, through March 31, 2017.
	
	
	Petitioner provided the auditor with financial statements, purchase invoices, tax accrual worksheets, tax worksheets, general ledgers, and a chart of accounts.
	
	
	Due to the volume of documents and consideration of the procedure from the previous audit, the auditor used a sample procedure to conduct the audit. Petitioner’s documentation reliably separated taxed and non-taxed populations. The auditor evaluated purchases through five separate sample groups: inventory, taxed labor, non-taxed labor, taxed assets and expenses, and non-taxed assets and expenses.
	
	
	The auditor selected sample purchases from the groups and reviewed supporting documents, including purchase invoices and work papers. In multiple sample exams, he scheduled taxable purchases for which Petitioner did not remit tax to the vendor or accrue tax to remit to the Comptroller. He also scheduled credits where applicable.
	
	
	On May 28, 2021, Staff issued Petitioner a Texas Notification of Audit Results assessing tax and interest. Staff waived penalty.
	
	
	Petitioner timely requested redetermination of the audit.
	
	
	Staff amended the audit by removing items for exempt manufacturing equipment, exempt railcars, nontaxable railroad switching services, and exempt waste removal services. The auditor also agreed to credit taxed transactions for exempt manufacturing equipment, new construction labor, and nontaxable consulting services. The amendments to the assessment resulted in a credit of tax and corresponding interest.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to adjust the amended assessment and referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On May 6, 2025, ALJ Keneshia Washington issued an Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits and Setting Pre-Hearing Requirements for a hearing to be held in person at the SOAH offices in Austin.
	
	
	On May 7, 2025, Staff issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing. The notice contained a statement of the date, time, and location of the hearing; the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 17, 2025, the ALJ issued an order granting Petitioner’s unopposed motion to convert the hearing to written submission and cancelling the in-person hearing.
	
	
	The record closed October 20, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notices of the hearings. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code §§ 111.009; .105.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	A “sale” includes a transfer of title or possession of tangible personal property or the performance of a taxable service when done or performed for consideration. Tex. Tax Code § 151.005(1), (3).
	
	
	All components of the sale of taxable personal property are part of the total sales price and are taxable, and the total amount for which a taxable item is sold, leased, or rented includes a service that is a part of the sale. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.007(a), (b).
	
	
	Real property repair and remodeling services are taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0101(a)(13).
	
	
	With certain exceptions, real property repair and remodeling means the repair, restoration, remodeling, or modification of an improvement to real property. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0047(a).
	
	
	The term “contractor” includes any person who makes improvements to real property as part of periodic and scheduled maintenance of nonresidential real property. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(2).
	
	
	For operational and functional improvements to realty, maintenance means scheduled, periodic work that is necessary to sustain or support safe, efficient, continuous operations, or to prevent the decline, failure, lapse, or deterioration of the improvement. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7).
	
	
	Taxable real property services that are described by Texas Administrative Code § 3.356 (relating to Real Property Service) do not qualify as maintenance. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7).
	
	
	Maintenance does not include work to remodel, modify, upgrade, perform major repair, or restore, even if the work is scheduled or periodic. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7).
	
	
	As it relates to maintenance, the term “scheduled” means anticipated and designated to occur within a given time period or production level. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7)(A).
	
	
	As it relates to maintenance, the term “periodic” means ongoing or continual or at least occurring at intervals of time or production that are reasonably predictable. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7)(B).
	
	
	The scheduled shutdown or turnaround of a manufacturing or processing plant is considered to be maintenance within the meaning of this definition. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(a)(7)(C).
	
	
	Maintenance on real property is a nontaxable service. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(d)(2).
	
	
	To qualify a purchase as nontaxable real property maintenance, a service provider’s customer must prove by way of maintenance schedules or work orders or other similar forms of evidence that the services meet the definition of maintenance on real property that is stated in subsection (a)(7) of this section. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(d)(2)(A).
	
	
	If the service provider does not have a written contract, but is only hired on a per job basis, then the service provider must presume that the service is repair or restoration and must therefore collect tax. Id. If the customer has documentation to prove that the service qualifies as maintenance, then the customer may issue to the service provider an exemption certificate in lieu of paying tax or provide the documentation required to overcome the presumption. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(d)(2)(A).
	
	
	The certificate must state that the labor is maintenance as defined in subsection (a)(7) of this section, rather than repair or restoration as defined in subsection (a)(13) and (15) of this section, and that the customer is liable for any additional tax that is due in the event that the comptroller determines that a taxable service was performed. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(d)(2)(A).
	
	
	Previous Comptroller’s Decisions interpreting the meaning of the terms scheduled and periodic held that scheduled work is arranged in advance rather than on an as-needed basis and that work that is prompted by a subjective judgment is not periodic. See GATX Terminals Corp. v. Rylander, 78 S.W.3d 630, 635 (Tex. App.—Austin 2002, no pet.), referencing Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 30,911 (1999), 28,468 (1992).
	
	
	The scheduled shutdown or turnaround of a manufacturing or processing plant is generally considered to be maintenance of real property, but repairs or restoration performed during a scheduled, periodic shutdown are considered major repair if performed on improvements that were not operational when the shutdown occurred. See Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 40,239, 40,911 (2004).
	
	
	Working on a unit during the same month in two subsequent years does not per se support a determination that the work was scheduled and periodic; however, the Comptroller may allow minor variances due to such things as severe weather; yet wider variances may support an indication that the services were not scheduled or periodic. State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document No. 200710980L (October 8, 2007).
	
	
	There are no specific requirements for the amount of time or production between turnarounds. STAR Document No. 202407003M (July 1220, 2024).
	
	
	Although the intervals between turnarounds must be reasonably predictable, turnarounds may often be delayed or pushed back. For example, a functioning refinery may push back a scheduled turnaround to maintain production after other refineries have been damaged by a hurricane. STAR Document No. 202407003M (July 1220, 2024).
	
	
	To support a contention that purchases are scheduled and periodic turnaround services, a taxpayer should provide supporting documentation such as scope of work documents, project plans, turnaround schedules, vendor contracts, invoices, and Authorization for Expenditures (AFEs), which are internal approvals for expenses from a taxpayer’s capital expenditure budget. STAR Document No. 202407003M (July 1220, 2024).
	
	
	The Comptroller shall refund or credit tax if he determines that tax was unlawfully or erroneously collected. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a).
	
	
	When a refund claim is filed, the claimant is arguing that tax was “unlawfully or erroneously collected,” and the burden of proof rests on the claimant to prove the matter asserted, since the refund claim is based on documents in the claimant’s possession. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 43,649 and 43,665 (2006).
	
	
	The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b).
	
	
	A taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012).
	
	
	A taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,078 (2015).
	
	
	Bare assertions are insufficient to demonstrate error in the audit. See Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.—Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); Comptroller’s Decision No. 114,880 (2019).
	
	
	Summary schedules and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012).
	
	
	Staff met its burden to establish that Petitioner purchased taxable real property repair and remodeling services.
	
	
	Petitioner has not met its evidentiary burden to show that the services constituted scheduled and periodic maintenance that would be excludable from tax.
	
	
	Petitioner’s contention should be denied.
	
	
	Petitioner has not provided sufficient documentation to support any additional credits.
	
	
	The ALJ recommends that the amended assessment be upheld.
	


 

Signed                                     

November 25, 2025.

 

ALJ Signature:

Keneshia Washington

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 



 

 


ENDNOTES



[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.101(e) and (f).
subjects: MAINTENANCE OF REAL PROPERTY-SCHEDULED AND PERIODIC REQUIREMENTS AND DEFINITION
